coredata.dc:title: Cu0-doped graphitic carbon quantum dots for rapid electrochemical sensing of glyphosate herbicide in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Adeyemi Oladipo, Akeem
coredata.dc:description: A widely used herbicide, glyphosate (Glyp) is a major pollutant in the environment and can harm human health. To combat this, an electrochemical sensor has been developed using Cu0-doped graphitic carbon quantum dots (Cu0-OGQ) to detect trace amounts of Glyp in water, agricultural produce, vegetables, and fruits. The electrochemical behaviour of Cu0-OGQ was investigated using techniques such as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Cu0-OGQ has an electroactive area of 0.428 cm2, which is roughly 7 times greater than a bare glassy carbon electrode (0.0612 cm2) and 1.35 times larger than OGQ's (0.318 cm2). The developed Glyp sensor demonstrated under optimal experimental conditions a high sensitivity of 179.7 μA ng/mL and a low detection limit (LOD) of 2.02 ng/mL (S/N = 10) in a broad concentration range of 0.01 μM to 40 μM. Based on selectivity experiments, the sensor has the potential to detect Glyp even in the presence of similar pesticides and other interferences like glucose, ascorbic acid, and various metal ions. The Cu0-OGQ sensor exhibited a recovery rate between 90 and 102 % in real sample detection of Glyp and maintained 95 % performance even after 30 days. Thus, it can be a reliable, selective, and stable tool for environmental analysis.

head.ce:title: Cu0-doped graphitic carbon quantum dots for rapid electrochemical sensing of glyphosate herbicide in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymeric Materials Research Laboratory, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, Eastern Mediterranean University, TR North Cyprus, Famagusta via Mersin 10, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymeric Materials Research Laboratory, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Arts and Science, Eastern Mediterranean University, TR North Cyprus, Famagusta via Mersin 10, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A widely used herbicide, glyphosate (Glyp) is a major pollutant in the environment and can harm human health. To combat this, an electrochemical sensor has been developed using Cu0-doped graphitic carbon quantum dots (Cu0-OGQ) to detect trace amounts of Glyp in water, agricultural produce, vegetables, and fruits. The electrochemical behaviour of Cu0-OGQ was investigated using techniques such as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Cu0-OGQ has an electroactive area of 0.428 cm2, which is roughly 7 times greater than a bare glassy carbon electrode (0.0612 cm2) and 1.35 times larger than OGQ's (0.318 cm2). The developed Glyp sensor demonstrated under optimal experimental conditions a high sensitivity of 179.7 μA ng/mL and a low detection limit (LOD) of 2.02 ng/mL (S/N = 10) in a broad concentration range of 0.01 μM to 40 μM. Based on selectivity experiments, the sensor has the potential to detect Glyp even in the presence of similar pesticides and other interferences like glucose, ascorbic acid, and various metal ions. The Cu0-OGQ sensor exhibited a recovery rate between 90 and 102 % in real sample detection of Glyp and maintained 95 % performance even after 30 days. Thus, it can be a reliable, selective, and stable tool for environmental analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The alarmingly increasing volume of annual worldwide generation of agro-based biomass wastes as a consequence of agricultural expansion in the aftermath of population growth and rising standards of living is becoming a threat. Because rotten agro-biomass releases leachate, methane, and additional micropollutants, inefficient waste agricultural biomass management contributes to climate change and landfill pollution. To tackle this issue, it is highly appealing to convert waste biomass into valuable products [1,2].

Biowastes-derived carbon materialshave recently attracted muchinterest due to their low cost particularly lignocellulosic-based biomass, abundant bio-precursors, easy accessibility, and the distinctive characteristics (varied functional groups, adequate pore volumes, and various microstructures) ofcarbon-derived products, which have been employed for diverse bioenvironmental applications.

Oladipo et al. [3], for example, reportedly converted banana peel into high-performance biochar for the remediation of heavy metal-contaminatedwater. Rabbani et al. [4] synthesised high-quality porous carbon material from discarded watermelon peels for use as electrode materials in energy storage supercapacitors. Also, in a recent work published by Mehmandoust et al. [5], the authors reviewed the applicability of biomass-derived carbon compounds for electrochemical sensing applications. Because of their micro-mesopore structure, high surface area, high electrical conductivity, and outstanding chemical stability; porous carbons generated from biomass have been proven as a viable matrix for coating bare electrodes for electrochemical sensing [6–9].

Waste orange peel is one of the most generally available and collected agricultural wastes that could be utilised as a carbon precursor when compared to other routinely discarded agro-waste biomass [10–14]. According to a recent study published by Michael-Igolima et al. [15], global orange output is expected to be 60 million tonnes per year, while annual orange peel waste production is 32 million tonnes. This is consistent with the US Department of Agriculture's global market research report [16], which stated that global orange output for 2022/23 is predicted at 47.5 million, with the peel accounting for 20 % of the orange.

Herein, to contribute to biomass utilisation, orange peel-derived graphitic carbon quantum dots (OGQ) were synthesised and used as a modifier for fabricating a Cu0-doped-OGQ-based electrochemical sensing platform for rapid detection of Glyphosate (Glyp) herbicide. The growing global demand for food has resulted in an alarmingly high rate of herbicide use. Among these herbicides, Glyp is one of the most frequently used post-emergence herbicides that are not selective [17] with applications in agriculture (corn fields, soybeans, canola, and cotton), gardening, lawn, forestry, industrial weed control, and aquatic habitats.

According to several regulatory agencies, including the European Food Safety Authority, the World Health Organisation, and the United States Environmental Protection Agency [18], there are no risks to people's health when Glyp or aminomethylphosphonic acid, its main degradation product, is applied as directed. However, continuing use of excessive Glyp increases the possibility of residue accumulation in foods, water, and soil samples. Glyp levels in drinking water are limited to 700 μg/L in the United States and 0.1 μg/L in the European Union [19]. According to the National Pesticide Information Centre [20,21], the Glyp reference dose for humans is 1.75 mg/kg/day. Glyp and its residues are safe at quantities within regulatory acceptable limits; however, exposure to doses above the permitted levels can result in neurotoxicity, liver, kidney, and heart damage, shock, loss of consciousness, or breathing difficulties [17,18,22].

As a result, monitoring Glyp levels in soil, drinking water, fruits, and food samples has become a necessity to guarantee that the maximum permitted level is met. Several methods for Glyp analysis have been developed, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography, spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis, and so on. These techniques perform well in terms of selectivity, sensitivity, and repeatability; but, they have several limitations, including extensive sample preparation, the need for expertise, time-consuming analysis, and expensive instruments. Electroanalytical methods [23] are promising alternatives to chromatographic approaches for detecting Glyp. There are several benefits of using an electrochemical sensor platform: (i) low cost (ii) detection limits with appropriate sensitivity (iii) for field deployment, electrochemical sensors can be miniaturized (iv) the capacity to do direct analysis without pre-treatment processes and generate minimal waste (v) rapid analysis time with high reproducibility.

Recently, research has focused on the development of electrochemical sensors for detecting and monitoring Glyp in environmental samples. However, because of the low electroactivity of Glyp within the readily obtainable potential range, electrochemical detection of Glyp without modifying the electrodesis difficult [24,25]. To address this issue, techniques based on indirect electrochemical sensing have been developed for the detection of Glyp, employing various electrodes made of noble metals such as gold, platinum, and others [26]. It should be noted that noble metal electrodes are susceptible to surface fouling due to significant irreversible adsorption of Glyp carboxylic and amino moieties [25], and some researchers found low current signals. Noori et al. [26], for example, employed a gold electrode to directly detect Gly in tap water and reported a detection limit of only 2 μM.

As a result, electrode modifications are required for Glyp detection to improve quantifiable signals. Copper is preferred over other metals for electrode improvement because of its outstanding catalytic performance, low resistance, and strong electrochemical activity. In previous research [24–28], several sensors based on copper electrodes for the detection of GLY showed an increase in peak current, which was partly attributable to Glyp complexation with cupric ions. The carboxyl, amine, and phosphate groups in Glyp readily react with Cu2+ to produce a stable Cu2+−Glyp complex, which serves as the foundation for the sensing mechanism of copper-based electrodes.

Here is a brief discussion of a few of the copper-based electrochemical sensors for Glyp detection. Zhang et al. [24] synthesized a Cu-based hydroxide-doped-graphene nanomaterial as an electrochemical sensor for detecting trace amounts of Glyp in water samples. They reported a higher detection limit of 1 × 10−9 M with outstanding anti-interference capabilities and a reasonable recovery of 97.6–108.1 % in surface water. For the electrochemical detection of Glyp in tap water samples by differential pulse voltammetry, Setznagl and Cesarino [27] modified a glassy carbon electrode with Cu-reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles. According to the scientists, Glyp was successfully detected and quantified in water samples with a detection limit of 0.19 μM, and the significant redox peaks were attributed to copper ion oxidation and reduction.

Researchers have also focused on the use of quantum dots as opto-electrochemical sensors [29–31] because of their distinctive electrical properties that differ from the components of their bulk structure. In addition to having a large number of electrochemical sites for target detection, quantum dots also have unique quantum mechanical features, a high surface area, and a rapid electron transfer rate, all of which improve response times and decrease detection limits.

To utilize orange peel biowastes, a novel nanostructure composed of orange peel-derived carbon quantum dots with graphitic structure doped with zero-valent Cu0 (Cu0-OGQ) was prepared using the hydrothermal synthesis approach in this study. The glassy carbon electrode was modified with Cu0-OGQ for the detectionand quantification of the Glyp herbicide in environmental samples (agricultural soils, vegetables, and water samples). The combination of OGQ's high electrical conductivity and unusual electronic properties with Cu0′s numerous electrocatalytic sites and high specific surface area resulted in an electrode material with enhanced conductivity, electrocatalytic activity, and higher current signal for sensing applications.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to assess the electrocatalytic behaviour and charge transfer resistance of the modified electrode. The novelty of the present research lies in the fact that an electrochemical sensor for herbicide detection was developed using graphitic orange peel-derived biocarbon doped with zero-valent copper for the first time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Reagents, materials and instrumentation
ce:section.ce:para: All of the chemicals utilised in the present research are of analytical grade and were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). The chemicals are potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, Nafion (5 % w/w), potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), copper nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), glyphosate, and methanol. Monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4·H2O), NaCl, and potassium chloride (KCl) were used to prepare the phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution as supporting electrolyte, with an ionic strength of 0.1 M. Orange peels that had been discarded were collected from the well-known Thursday Market in Famagusta, North Cyprus. Utilising a Perkin-Elmer Japan FTIR-8700 spectrophotometer, the functional groups were examined. The Rigaku Ultima IV machine (λ = 1.541 nm; 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Kα radiation) was used to conduct the XRD. SEM FEI Quanta 200 with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) capabilities was used to record the morphology. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with Hitachi HighTech HT7700 (Tokyo, Japan) and Belsorp mini II (Microtrac Bel Corp., Japan) was used for the N2 adsorption/desorption studies. The PHI 5000 Versa Probe III was used to collect the XPS data. The Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical, UK) was used to confirm the size of Cu0-OGQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of zero-valent copper nanoparticles (Cu0)
ce:section.ce:para: Cu0 was synthesized using modified procedures that had previously been described by [32,33]. The synthesis of Cu0 began with the preparation of an orange peel extract that was utilized as a capping and reducing agent. Firstly, orange peels were washed in tap water to eliminate dust before drying at 90 °C for 18 h. The samples of dried orange peel were crushed, then pulverizedinto small particles and filtered using a mesh sieve with a size 10 opening. 8 g of the powdered orange peel was combined with 250 mL of distilled water in an Erlenmeyer flask to produce the orange peel extract, which was then heated for 45 min at 70 °C and filtered.

Secondly, to synthesize Cu0 nanoparticles, 30 mL of orange peel extract was gradually added to 75 mL of a 20 mM copper nitrate solution while being agitated in a nitrogen atmosphere. At 80 °C, the solution was agitated for 45 min. The reaction vessel's solution changed from yellowish to black-brown after 45 min of stirring, demonstrating the formation of Cu0 nanoparticles. The Cu0 nanoparticles were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained precipitate was washed with a distilled water–ethanol mixture and then vacuum-dried at 65 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of orange-peel derived graphitic carbon quantum dots (OGQ)
ce:section.ce:para: The top-down hydrothermal synthesis method previously reported [31,34,35] was modified to produce the graphitic quantum dots (OGQ) utilising pulverized orange peel as a carbon precursor material. A mixture of 10 mL of ethanol, 20 mL of concentrated H2SO4, and 50 mL of deionized water was added to 5 g of powdered orange peel, and the mixture was magnetically agitated at room temperature until it turned dark brown. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heatedfor 5 hat 180 °C. After reaching room temperature, the resultant dark suspension was rinsed with distilled water to eliminate the acid. The final steps were centrifuging the product at 4000 rpm for 20 min, dialysing it for 7 h at neutral pH with 32 mm benzoylated dialysis tubing, washing it with an ethanol-acetone solution, and allowing it to dry under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu0-OGQ
ce:section.ce:para: With the addition of 0.5–1.5 g of the synthesised Cu0, the sameprocess outlined in section 2.3 was used to produce Cu0-OGQ. 0.95 g of Cu0 is the optimal amount of Cu0 to produce the effective Cu0-OGQ, according to a preliminary evaluation of electrochemical behaviour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the Cu0-OGQ/GCE electrochemical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: First, a mirror-like surface was achieved by sequentially polishing the bareglassy carbon electrode (GCE) with 0.3 and 0.05 μm Al2O3 slurry. The electrode was then carefully cleaned with deionized water and ethanol before being left to dry. The Cu0-OGQ-based ink was made in a 1.5 mL cap vial with 0.25–2.0 mg of Cu0-OGQ as the modifying agent, 1 mL of an ethanol–water mixture as the solvent and 10 μL of a Nafion (5 % w/w) solution as the binder. To produce a homogenous mixture that could be used to modify the bare GCE, the mixture in the vial was ultrasonically blended for 20 min. 5 μL of the ink was uniformly drop casted onto the surface of bare GCE and left to dry under an infra-red lamp for 20 min then recoated with ink for the second time. The Cu0-OGQ/GCE electrode was employed as an electrochemical sensor for Glyp detection. The same procedure was used for the preparation of OGQ/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of vegetables and fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: Carrots, tomatoes, cauliflower, chickpeas, soybeans, cucumber, and maize were purchased from Famagusta's well-known Thursday market in North Cyprus to test for the presence of Glyp in samples of fruits and vegetables. In a blender, 6.0 g of each fruit and vegetable were crushed before being added one at a time to 30 mL of the PBS/methanol extraction solution (70/30 %, v/v), and vortexing for 10 min. Then, prepared solutions of vegetable and fruit extracts were spiked with various Glyp concentrations to produce samples for Glyp quantitative detection. The solution was then centrifuged for 20 min at 3000 rpm. 1 mL of the supernatant was added to 4 mL of PBS (0.1 M pH 6.5) and the mixture was then filtered using 0.22 μm filters. Each spiked sample had final Glyp concentrations ranging from 10 ng/mL to 100 ng/mL. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), using the standard addition method, was used to estimate the Glyp concentration.

Note: Further experiments were conducted to determine the impact of adding the analyte to the actual samples before pre-treatment, centrifugation, and filtration steps. The average results obtained revealed no significant differences when compared to the method outlined above. These findings suggest that the additional step of adding the analyte to the samples before pre-treatment does not affect the results of the analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of agricultural soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: Two soil samples, black garden soil and agricultural soil from a cornfield, were collected at two distinct locations at a depth of 0 to 20 cm. They were then air-dried, homogenised, crushed, and sieved via a 2 mm mesh. A specific amount of soil samples was then weighed, added to a conical flask containingtwenty-five milliliters of distilled water, homogeneously mixed, and let to stand for 12 h for sedimentation ofthe dirty layer. Then each sample was filtered before being collected in a volumetric flask with a capacity of 50 mL. Later, the samples were diluted to the up-to-the-markusing PBS (0.1 M pH 6.5) after being spiked with various Glyp concentrations to produce samples for Glyp quantitative detection. The standard addition method was utilized for quantifying the Glyp concentration using differential pulse voltammograms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were obtained from five distinct sources in various parts of Northern Cyprus: river from the Kantara region, lake from Güzelyurt, RO water servicing Eastern Mediterranean University, groundwater collected from a 70 m deep well from the Karpaz agro-region, and ordinary home tap water. In a clean cell, 5.0 mL of 0.1 M PBS with a pH of 6.5 was added to5.0 mL of the appropriate water samples before spiking with different Glyp concentrations without any other pre-treatment. After recording the voltammograms for each sample, the amount of Glyp was estimated using the conventional addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: A three-electrode electrochemical workstation potentiostat/galvanostat (CS310, Corrtest Instrument, China) assembled with a 10 mL electrochemical cell was used for the electroanalytical studies. Platinum wire, the Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) electrode bought from PalmSens B.V. (Netherlands), and the Cu0-OGQ/GCE or OGQ/GCE were used as the counter, reference, and working electrodes. The glassy carbon working electrode (GCE) has a 3 mm diameter. The entire electrochemical research was carried out in N2-saturated conditions. CV and DPV techniques were used to examine bare and modified electrodes' electrochemical characteristics. With a scan rate of 50 mV/s, CV analyses were carried out between −0.8 V and 0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl. DPV was acquired at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.5). In a solution of 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- (1:1) containing 0.1 M KCl, measurements of EIS with an amplitude of 50 mV and a frequency range of 1 Hz–100 kHz were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters optimization and analytical curve for Glyp detection with Cu0-OGQ/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: By adjusting the pH between 3.0 and 9.0 in a concentration of 5 × 10−6 mol/L, the impact of electrolyte pH on the electrochemical behaviour of Glyp was investigated. With 5 × 10−6 mol/L of Glyp in the optimised pH, the effect of the scan rate at 25 to 200 mV/s was investigated. The analytical curve for Glyp was obtained at 10.0 × 10−9 to 50.0 × 10−6 mol/L. The analytical curve was used to compute the limitof detection (LOD = 3Sb/m) and quantification (LOQ = 10Sb/m); where m is the slope value of the analytical curve and Sb is the standard deviation of six blank measurements (0.1 M PBS pH = 6.5) [23,36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference, specificity, reproducibility, repeatability, and stability studies
ce:section.ce:para: Three common pesticides [Thiram (Thi), carbofuran (Car), and acetochlor (Ace)] at a ratio of 1 Glyp: 30 pesticides; aminomethylphosphonic acid (AMPA), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA) and a variety of metal ions (K+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, SO4 2-, Cl-, PO4 3- and NO3 –) at concentrations 50 times higher than Glyp (2.5 × 10−6 mol/L) were chosen as the interference species to test the sensor's ability to recognise Glyp. The DPV voltammograms were obtained after the interferents were added. Eightelectrodes fabricatedunder the same conditions were used to study the reproducibility of the modified electrode under optimal conditions. The sensor electrode's operational stability and repeatability were assessed using 5 repeated studies after the electrodes were stored in the ambient environment for 30 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of Cu0-OGQ
ce:section.ce:para: Cu0-OGQ's electrochemical performance is influenced by its physicochemical characteristics. Here, the functional groups, specific surface area, crystallographic information, elemental composition, and chemical composition of the Cu0-OGQ are clarified.

By using FTIR spectroscopy, the functional groups on the surface of the Cu0-OGQ were investigated. The broad peak at around 3185–3523 cm−1 and the narrow band at 2868–2900 cm−1 in Fig. 1 a, respectively, corresponded to N–H/O–H and C–H stretching vibrations [31,38–40]. The stretching vibrations of C = O, C = C, COO–, C–C, and C–N, respectively, correspond to the absorption peaks at 1714 cm−1 – 1378 cm−1 [41,42]. According to [42], the C-O-C is present in Cu0-OGQ because of the peak at 1115 cm−1. According to [40,41], the peaks at 1089 cm−1, 1036 cm−1, and 620–485 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of N–Cu, N–Cu–N, and lattice vibrational of Cu–O, demonstrating the formation of Cu complexes. Bands attributed to –Cu– stretching are not present in the OGQ spectra. The FTIR data reveal that the graphitic carbon dot contains oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups and that the carbonization process results in the formation of several unsaturated carbon bonds.

The degree of graphitic defects and structural disorder may be determined from the Raman spectra of Cu0-OGQ and OGQ in the samples (Fig. 1b). Three distinctive peaks, which correspond to the D band, G band, and 2D band, were seen at 1345 cm−1, 1590 cm−1, and 2684 cm−1.

Notably, a Raman active photonic oscillation assigned to Ag and possibly caused by the presence of Cu-O [43] in the sample is found at 298 cm−1 in the Cu0-OGQ spectrum. As can be seen in Fig. 1b, both doped and undoped OGQ exhibit a G-band (IG) intensity that is greater than a D-band (ID) intensity, supporting the existence of a properly aligned graphitic structure within the samples [4,44,45]. Calculations reveal that the ID/IG ratios of the Cu0-OGQ and OGQ are, respectively, 0.684 and 0.616. Due to the incorporation of Cu0 within the graphitic network, there was a clear increase in structural disorder with doping [46]. The development of well-ordered, highly concentrically layered graphene within the carbon framework is confirmed by the presence of a 2D band [47]. As a result, the Cu0-OGQ has improved electrochemical characteristics and very good charge-transfer properties.

To further characterise the surface functional state and constituents of the Cu0-OGQ, XPS measurements were performed (Fig. 1c). The Cu0-OGQ containsC (69.8 %), N (2.98 %), O (25.6 %), and Cu (1.63 %), according to the full XPS spectrum, which shows four characteristic peaksat 286.2 eV, 402.3 eV, 530.9 eV, and 935.8 eV. According to Fig.S1, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum had three peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.7 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to C = C/C-C, C-O, C-N/C = N and O-C = O, respectively [4]. C–N and N–H are represented by the N1s peaks at 399.8 and 401.8 eV, respectively. The peak at 399.8 eV denotes the chelation of N to copper, which is also shown in the FTIR (1089 cm−1 and 1036 cm−1 band). According to [41], the peaks of O1s at 531.3 and 533.2 eV, respectively, are associated with C = O and C-OH/C-O-C. Cu (II) is present in the sample because the spin–orbit splitting of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 corresponds to the peaks of Cu 2p at 932.8, 944.5, and 953.3 eV, respectively. Particle size analysis was used to determine the size of Cu0-OGQ particles, which range from 3 to 9 nm, with most of the dots being between 6 and 8 nm.

The XRD data for Cu0-OGQ is shown in Fig. 1d, and the diffraction peak at 23.8° with a d-spacing of 0.368 nm matchesthe carbon lattice's (002) crystal plane [4,31,41]; and that of carbon dot reported by [48]. However, other peaks at 66.6°, 57.9°, 50.8°, and 41.1° are associated with (220), (202), (−202), and (111) planes of cubic CuO, respectively [49,50], this matches the ICSD 98–006-9757 standard card number. The diffraction peak at 28.9° corresponds to the(110) plane of cubic Cu2O [43]. Based onthe crystalline planes (002) and (110), the lattice parameters a and c of OGQ and Cu0-OGQ were calculated.

The lattice parameter a
in OGQ and Cu0-OGQ are respectively 2.21 Åand 2.37 Å. And 9.48 Å in OGQ and 9.49 Å (Cu0-OGQ) are the lattice parameter values c . Thelattice parameters in the Cu0 doped OGQ are marginally higher than those in the undoped OGQ, which is probably due to the lattice stress triggered by the introduction of Cu0 dopant into the lattice site/surface of the graphitic carbon dots network and the resulting disruption of thestructure inducedby the formation of oxygen vacancies [51]. Using the Debye-Scherrer formula, D = 0.9λ/(β·cos θ), where D is the average crystallite size, the X-ray wavelength is λ, β represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) and θ is the Bragg diffraction angle, an average crystallite size of 3.89 nm was determined for the synthesized Cu0-OGQ and 4.23 nm for OGQ. It is possible that the formation of oxygen vacancies and the resulting increase in lattice stress, which inhibited crystal growth, are what caused the reduction in crystallite size on Cu0 doping.

The microstructure of Cu0-OGQ and OGQ was analyzed using the TEM, and the results are shown in Fig.S2. The TEM images revealed that both OGQ and Cu0-OGQ consist of roughly spherical and highly uniform particles, with an average diameter size of 5.15 ± 0.25 nm for OGQ and 6.56 ± 0.38 nm for Cu0-OGQ. The magnified TEM images (inset in Fig.S2) showed that the morphologies of both Cu0-OGQ and OGQ were similar, indicating that the incorporation of Cu0 onto the OGQ had no significant effect on the morphology of the OGQ but slightly increased the particle size. The data obtained from TEM analyses corroborates with the BET measurements. It is observed that the OGQ has a smaller particle size than the Cu0-OGQ. This implies that the number of particles per unit volume is higher. Consequently, the total surface area increases, leading to a higher specific surface area of 761.9 m2/g for OGQ than for Cu0-OGQ, which has a specific surface area of 756.6 m2/g. While some of the particles of both Cu0-OGQ and OGQ appeared amorphous, most of the particles appeared crystalline, likely due to the graphitization. These morphologies and uniformity of the graphitic carbon dots are consistent with those reported elsewhere [52,53]. However, due to the lack of access to high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), lattice fringes could not be clearly noticed.

Fig. 2 a displays the Cu0-OGQ and OGQ nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry's classification, the isotherms show micropore (pore size ≤ 2 nm) filling in the low-pressure zone, and an open hysteresis loop from 0.4 to 0.97 of P/P0 shows the presence of numerous mesopores (pore size: 2–50 nm) [54]. The calculated BET-specific surface area of Cu0-OGQ is 757.6 m2/g, which is marginally closer to the 761.9 m2/g determined from OGQ. For Cu0-OGQ and OGQ, the total pore volume is 0.598 cm3/g and 0.667 cm3/g; the results are consistent with the graphitic carbon dot reported by [48]. According to a slit-pore model's examination of the isotherms using density functional theory, the pore size ranges from 1 nm to 7 nm, with large pores predominating in Cu0-OGQ (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of bare GCE and modified working electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: CV and EIS were used as an electrochemical approach to investigate the electroactive area, the electrochemical characteristics, and the impact of Cu0 doping on OGQ. The CV curves of unmodified and modified electrodes were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6.5) alone and 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl without Glyp at the same scan rate of 50 mV/s, are presented in Fig. 3 . No redox peak was observed in PBS for the bare GCE (Fig. 3a).

An obvious response peak was observed after the GCE was modified by OGQ; this may have been a result of the numerous electroactive sites on the highly conductive graphitic carbonaceous OGQ and the increased effective area of the OGQ/GCE, which synergistically enhanced the redox activity of the electrode and amplified the current response. The observedanodic current response was more pronounced for the Cu0-OGQ/GCE, demonstrating that the doping of Cu0 improved the active sites, conductivity, and electrochemical performance of the OGQ. Additionally, the apparent two peaks for Cu0-OGQ/GCE in the anodic direction are most likely linked to the oxidation of Cu0 to Cu2+, which is consistent with the research published by [37].

The cyclic voltammogram of the bare GCE in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution (Fig. 3b) revealed two clearly defined redox peaks with an anodic peak-to-cathodic peak interval (ΔEp ) of ∼ 350 mV and anodic peak current of roughly 8.65 µA. The anodic peak current increased to 14.8 µA and 22.5 µA, respectively, after the GCE surface was modified using OGQ and Cu0-OGQ. It should be noted that the ΔEp values for the modified GCE electrodes are lower, with the ΔEp for Cu0-OGQ/GCE having been decreased to 300 mV in particular. This demonstrates a better electron-transfer process for the modified GCE electrodes. Cu0-OGQ/GCE exhibits a peak in the cathodic direction at −0.42 V, which can be attributed to the reduction of [FeIII(CN)6]3− to [FeII(CN)6]4−.

The findings show that the anodic peak current of Cu0-OGQ was enhanced by ∼ 1.5–2.6 times in comparison to the unmodified GCE and OGQ/GCE. It should be noted that two peaks are apparent in the anodic direction for Cu0-OGQ. According to earlier research [37,55], the [FeII(CN)6]4− to [FeIII(CN)6]3− oxidation is likely responsible for the peak at 0.11 V [27,56]. The second peak, which appears at 0.31 V is probably due to the oxidation of [FeIII(CN)6]3− to [FeIV(CN)6]2− [31]. The higher peak response exhibited by Cu0-OGQ indicates a higher electron transfer rate, which can be attributed to its better conductivity, more electroactive surface area, and catalytic sites. The incorporation of Cu0 into the OGQ framework resulted in lattice stress, structural defect, and the creation of oxygen vacancies, which synergistically improved electron transport and catalytic capabilities of Cu0-OGQ, as demonstrated by the Raman and XRD data.

Further research on the electrochemically active surface areas of unmodified and modified GCE was conducted using the CV method at scan rates ranging from 25 to 200 mV/s (Fig. 3c). Redox peak currents increased linearly with the increase in scan rate as well as with the square root of the scan rates (Fig. 3d). Ipa (μA) = 0.3266v 1/2 + 0.9063 (R2 = 0.997) and Ipc (μA) = –0.1808v 1/2 – 1.316 (R2 = 0.992) are the linear equations for the redox peak currents vs v 1/2 of the bare GCE electrode (Fig. 3d inset). Ipa (μA) = 2.286v 1/2 + 6.616 (R2 = 0.992) and Ipc (μA) = –1.266 v 1/2 – 9.379 (R2 = 0.989) are the linear equations of the redox peak currents vs v 1/2 of Cu0-OGQ (Fig. 3d). The ratio of the Ipa/Ipc values, is obtained to be roughly one for both the bare and modified electrodes, suggesting that the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox process occurring on the working electrode's surface is reversible [36]. Moreover, as Fig. 3c demonstrates, the electrochemical reversibility of the process as well as rapid electron transfer are supported by the independence of the ΔEp of the voltammograms from the scan rates.

The electroactive area of Cu0-OGQ is 3.082 × 10−3 cm2, which is around 7 times larger than that of bare GCE (4.403 × 10−4 cm2) and 1.35 times larger than that of OGQ (2.283 × 10−3 cm2), according to the Randles-Sevick equation (Eq.1). Cu0-OGQ's improved electrochemical performance is partially attributed to its greater electroactive area. Due to its superior physicochemical and electrochemical properties, Cu0-OGQ was used in subsequent experiments.

Where CFC is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, ip is the peak current (μA), n = 1, is the number of electrons participating in the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox reaction, A is the electrode's electroactive surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s), and v is the scan rate (mV/s).

The parameters Rs , Rct , Wo , and Cdl were calculated by simulating the Randles equivalent circuit (as shown in Fig. 4a inset) and the Nyquist plot using the experimental data. Since the electrolyte is the same, the data obtained (Table 1 ) show that the Rs valuesare almost equal. However, the smaller Rct value of the Cu0-OGQ/GCE corresponds to superior conductivity when compared to the bare GCE and OGQ/GCE electrodes. Also, when comparing Cu0-OGQ/GCE to the bareGCE and OGQ/GCE, the results showed a 450 % and 134 % increase in Cdl values. These findings suggest that the Cu0-OGQ's surface has more charges than OGQ. The observed increase in the Cdl valuemay also be explained by the presence of the dopant Cu0, which increases the electrochemical reactive area. The Cu0-OGQ/GCE sensor has a roughness factor (Rf ) that is ∼ 7 and ∼ 1.35 times more than that of the bare GCE and the OGQ/GCE, respectively, to enhance the interaction of the analyte with the electrode at the electrode-solution interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of Glyp using bare GCE and Cu0-OGQ
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical sensing behaviour was investigated by comparing the CV curves for the modified GCE electrodes in the Glyp sampling solutions (Fig. 4b). For the bare GCE electrode, it is clear from Fig. 3a that the bare GCE in 0.1 M PBS solution (blank) is a smooth curve devoid of any visible redox peaks. Notably, the OGQ/GCE shows two distinct redox peaks in comparison to bare GCE, which may be caused by the existence of several oxygen species (i.e. carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc.) [36,59] that may aid in the electrocatalysis process.

Similar redox peaks with increased response peak current are shown in the Cu0-OGQ/GCE compared to OGQ/GCE. There are also obvious splits in the anodic region, which may be caused by Cu0 electrooxidizing to Cu+/Cu2+ [27,28,37,59 27]. The oxidation peak currents of Cu0-OGQ/GCE (Fig. 4b) and OGQ/GCE (Fig. 4b inset) were seen to decrease upon the addition of 5.0 × 10−6 mol/L Glyp to the PBS electrolyte. Although the peak current of both electrodes decreased when Glyp was present, their sensing methods are distinct.

In comparison, there are clear differences between the voltammograms of Cu0-OGQ/GCE in PBS with Glyp and in PBS without Glyp. In Glyp, there is no apparent cathodic peak can be observed, and the peak attributed to Cu2+ (see Fig. 3a) has substantially decreased. The specific chelating groups in Glyp (C = O, P = O, and N-H) have a great affinity for copper ions, therefore when Glyp is present in the solution being tested for the Cu0-OGQ/GCE, the adsorbed Glyp would ultimately conjugate with copper to create stable complex [24]. The redox processes of the copper ions are restrained by these stable Cu-Glyp complexes [28,37,59], which restricts electron transport and causes the response current to decrease. This finding is in line with a research study by [24], in which it was shown that the Glyp molecules strongly retarded CuAl-LDH/Gr/GCE, causing peak currents to be reduced.

For OGQ/GCE in the presence of Glyp (Fig. 4b inset), even though Glyp is a non-electroactive molecule with donor groups (such as phosphonates, carboxylates, and amines) present, the protonated functional groups have chemical affinity for the oxygen-containing groups in OGQ. The adsorption of Glyp may be the cause of the reduction in the current response observed in OGQ/GCE. Note that the peak response current of Cu0-OGQ/GCE is lower than that of OGQ/GCE. The existence of an oxidation peak in OGQ/GCE indicates that OGQ's sensing mechanism for Glyp is probably multistage, including both electrooxidation and functional group adsorption. To clarify the sensing mechanisms, more research was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of variables for the detection of Glyp: DPV analysis with Cu0-OGQ/GCE and OGQ/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Important Glyp detection conditions were optimised to develop an efficient electrochemical sensing platform. The average findings of triplicate experimental runs are presented here.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of deposition potential and deposition time
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that the peak current intensity while performing DPV measurements is largely dependent on the deposition potential and deposition time. Without changing the pH of the sampling solution; the deposition potential in the range of −0.4 V to 0.8 V and the accumulation time from 25 to 250 s, were studied using the Cu0-OGQ/GCE and OGQ/GCE as the working electrodes. The difference between the peak current in the presence and absence of Glyp is represented by ΔIp.

For Cu0-OGQ/GCE, Glyp is negatively charged when it is in an acidic environment, so when the deposition voltage was negative, the peak current reduction ΔIp was less and increased accordingly (Fig. 5 a) toward positive voltage, which indicated that more Glyp was drawn to the electrode surface at an increasing deposition potential through the electrostatic force. The observed peak current dropped beyond 0.2 V, maybe due to partial dissolution of copper at higher potentials, as reported by [24,60]. As a result, 0.2 V was chosen as the optimum deposition potential for subsequent studies. For OGQ/GCE (Fig. 5a inset), a similar trend was noted; however, it stabilised at 0.6 V and reached an optimal deposition potential at 0.8 V.

According to Fig. 5b, the effect of Glyp's surface deposition time on the Cu0-OGQ/GCE electrode shows that ΔIp increases as deposition time increases. After 100 s, the increasing trend gradually levelled off, and after some time, the ΔIp began to decrease due to the electrode's adsorption saturation of Glyp. Herein, a deposition period of 100 s was deemed optimal. A deposition period of 150 s was found to be ideal for OGQ/GCE, as illustrated in the Fig. 5b inset.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pH and amount of electrode coating materials on GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of pH on the electrochemical behaviour of Glyp was studied by DPV under optimal conditions using both Cu0-OGQ/GCE and OGQ/GCE as working electrodes. Changes in the pH of the sampling solution have an impact on the Cu0-OGQ's surface potential as well as the charge characteristics of Glyp, a polyprotic acid having four pKa values: 10.6, 5.6, 2.6, and 2.0 [61,62]. The results (Fig. 5c) indicate a very low DPV response in the more acidic conditions, which may be caused by the protonation of the functional groups in the Glyp molecule, which lowers its capacity for complexation. The ΔIp value gradually increases as the pH value increases. Near pH 7, because of the depletion of protons, the negatively charged functional groups of Glyp emerged and thus coordinated with the copper ions in the modified electrode. Specifically, at pH = 6.5, the maximum peak intensity was observed and appeared stable until pH 7. Then, the DPV responses decreased beyond pH 7; it could be a result of increasing OH− and Glyp degradation in solutionwith alkaline pH [63].

A similar trend was observed for OGQ/GCE and the maximum peak intensity was observed at pH 7. Its regression equation Ep (V) = -0.0579 pH + 1.06 (R2 = 0.998) demonstrates the peak potential's linear dependence on pH (inset in Fig. 5c). The slope of 57.9 mV pH−1, which is near the theoretical value of 59 mV predicted by the Nernst equation, indicates that the total number of electrons as well as protons that participate in the redox process of the Glyp on the surface of the OGQ/GCE is equal [36,62]. The Glyp oxidation by OGQ/GCE is pHdependent, as evidenced by the anodic peak potential of Glyp shifting towards negative values as pH increases. The sensitivity of the sensor is affected by the amount of Cu0-OGQ and OGQ on the electrode surface. Fig. 5d illustrates that while ΔIp peaked at 1.5 mg/mL Cu0-OGQ, ΔIp steadily decreased as the amount of Cu0-OGQ increased, possibly due to material agglomeration. A comparable pattern was noted for OGQ, and the ideal concentration of OGQ on the GCE electrode surface was also determined to be 1.5 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for electrochemical detection of Glyp and kinetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative experiment was carried out to investigate and clarify the response mechanism for Cu0-OGQ/GCE and OGQ/GCE in more detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu0-OGQ/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: For 30 min, the as-synthesised Cu0-OGQ and OGQ particles were first suspended separately in aqueous solutions containing Glyp. The samples were then collected, air-dried, and characterized by FTIR. From the results, the spectraexhibitdistinct absorption peaks, as seen in Fig.S3. The Cu0-OGQ spectrumdisplaysprominent peaks at 974 and 1005 cm−1, which are attributed to the phosphonate moiety's P = O stretching vibration. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of the Cu–N bonds is assignedto the weak band at 462 cm−1, suggesting that Glyp was coordinated with the Cu0-OGQ. Therefore, the electrochemical process that occurred on the surface of Cu0-OGQ/GCE is governed by adsorption or stable complex formation (the main mechanism) that allows indirect electrochemical detection of Glyp. When OGQ-Glyp's spectrum is compared to OGQ's in Fig. 1a, the broad peak at about 3185–3523 cm−1 exhibits a decreased intensity. In contrast, the intensity of C = O (1714 cm−1) increased, suggesting that the surface adsorption process underlies OGQ-Glyp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OGQ/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the scan rate on Ip was utilised as a diagnostic tool to confirm the Glyp surface adsorption process onto OGQ, toestablish the electron transfer steps that occur on the electrode surface, and to elucidate the mechanism of the analyte's redox process. In this experiment, the effects of the scan rate (25 to 200 mV/s) on the electrochemical response of 1.0 × 10−9 mol/L Glyp in 0.1 M PBS at pH 6.5, employing OGQ/GCE working electrode, were examined by CV.

As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, the oxidation peaks are linearly proportional to the scan rate (v). According to [36,64], a surface-controlled process is indicated by the anodic peak current values being linearly proportional to the scan rate in the assessed range. In addition, the lack of a reduction peak suggests that this electrochemical process is irreversible [36,62]. The plot log of Ip vs. log of v (Fig. 6b) exhibits a linear relationship. The obtained slope (0.982) is closer to 1.0 and indicates that there is an adsorption stage [36], in which electroactive species migrate from the bulk phase to the interface layer of the OGQ/GCE working electrode and become adsorbed on the readily available pores, followed by electrooxidation process.

The kinetic parameters for OGQ [Γ: surface coverage (mol/cm2); H+: number of protons; n: number of involved electrons; kr : standard heterogeneous rate constant; α: charge transfer coefficient; and C: concentration (mol/cm3)] can be estimated experimentally according to Eqs. (2) − 5 [36,65,66] and using data from Fig. 6a-b.

The gas constant is R = 8.314 J/K mol, the temperature is T = 298 K, the Faraday constant is F = 96485C/mol, the scan rate is v = 200 mV/s, the charge transfer coefficient is α, which for irreversible reactions is 0.5, the charge consumed during the reaction is Q (in Coulomb, estimated as the area beneath the peak of Ip (A)/t (s)), C in (mol/cm3) is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–, and the electrode geometric area is A = 6.58 × 10−2 cm2.

Using Eq.4, the Γ of bare GCE and OGQ/GCE electrodes was calculated. Γ of 2.48 × 10−12 mol/cm2 and 1.22 × 10−11 mol/cm2 were obtained for GCE and OGQ/GCE, respectively. These results show that the OGQ/GCE surfaces absorbed around five times as much Glyp as the GCE, which is in line with the surface roughness factor. The estimated values for the electrooxidation of Glyp on the surface of OGQ/GCE under optimal experimental conditions are n = 1.90 and H+ = 2.14 (using the slopes of Ep vs. pH (Fig. 5c inset)); this indicates that two electrons andprotons were involved in the process. Using Eq.5, the experimental determination of the kr yielded a value of 1.63 × 10−4 cm/s and 2.17 × 10−4 cm/s for the OGQ/GCE and bare GCE, respectively. In comparison to the GCE, OGQ/GCE has a lower kr value, indicating a slower rate of electron transfer. Based on the EIS results, this is probably because OGQ/GCE has a larger charge transfer resistance than GCE.

Themechanism for the OGQ/GCE electrochemical sensor is proposed, which involves multiple stages, based on the results of the kinetic parameters mentioned above. On the surface of OGQ/GCE, the Glyp initially undergoes adsorption before electrooxidation. The protonation of the secondary amine's nitrogen atom in the Glyp in the first step, followed by the deprotonation of the hydroxyl functional, causes the formation of the deprotonated Glyp. In the second stage of electrocatalytic oxidation, which involved the transfer of two electrons and protons between the Glyp and the surface of the OGQ/GCE, acetic acid and cyanophosphonic acid were most likely generated, according to the path reported by [36,62]. The probable adsorption-chelation sensing mechanismfor Glyp by Cu0-OGQ/GCE and the electrooxidation of Glyp by OGQ/GCE are depicted in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limits of detection and quantification in terms of the analytical curve
ce:section.ce:para: By taking into account the improved characteristics of Cu0-OGQ/GCE and its superior sensing capability in the Glyp cyclic voltammetry test when compared to OGQ/GCE. Results of a quantitative analysis of the analyte Glyp using Cu0-OGQ/GCE via the optimised DPV conditions are presented in Fig. 7 . As seen in Fig. 7a, the DPV response peak current increases steadily as the concentration of Glyp increases from 10.0 × 10−9 mol/L to 50.0 × 10−6 mol/L, in line with research published by [36,62].

Other scientists, on the other hand, noticed a declining tendency in the DPV responses as the glyphosate concentrationincreased. For instance, [28] employed Ti3C2Tx/Cu-BTC as an electrochemical sensor to detect glyphosate and showed a declining trend in the DPV response as glyphosate concentration increased; the authors attributed the tendency to the generation of more glyphosate-Cu2+ complex. Similar to this, [67] observed that the trend in the maximal peak currents of the DPV decreased with increasing Glyp concentration.

The linear rangefrom 0.01 μM to 40 μM, with a LOD of 12 nM and a LOQ of 39.9 nM (S/N = 10), was expressed by the analytical plot, which was defined by the linear regression equation I (μA) = 1.063C (μM) + 20.83, with the R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 7a inset). When the findings from this study are compared with some previously published methodologies and electrochemical sensors, the electrochemical method exhibits a lower detection limit and a wider detection range for Glyp detection (Table 2 ). Notably, the LOD = 2.02 ng/mL found here (Table 2) demonstrates that the proposed Cu0-OGQ sensor permits the detection of Glyp amount at a concentration significantly lower than that of the maximum allowed in drinking water, as set by regulatory agencies including Health Canada [68], which sets 280 ng/mL; the American Environmental Protection Agency [69], which sets 700 ng/mL; 500 ng/mL set by the Minnesota Department of Health [70]; 900 ng/mL set by the World Health Organisation [71]; 1000 ng/mL set by Australian water quality agency [72] and 500 ng/mL set by the Brazilian National Health Surveillance Agency [73].

The LOD in this study is also lower than the 0.1–40 mg/kg limit set by the European Commission for Glyp in grains and food samples (such as rice, maize, and wheat) [74]. Since the proposed sensor is sensitive to very low levels of Glyp and exhibits a broader dynamic range, it is reliable and could potentially utilised to detect Glyp residues in various environmental samples.

COF: covalent organic framework; CP: carbon paper; CuONPs/N-Cdot: Copper oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots; LDH: Layered double hydroxides; Gr: graphene; Ti3C2Tx: Mxenes; Cu-BTC: copper benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability, anti-interference, and sensitivity of the Cu0-OGQ
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the analytical performance of the proposed Cu0-OGQ-based electrochemical sensor, studies of repeatability, precision, selectivity, and reproducibility, were carried out under optimal conditions. The DPV signal of 2.5 × 10−6 mol/L of Glyp was obtained for eight different electrodes made under identical conditions to examine reproducibility. The peak currents' relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.96 % (Fig. 7b), demonstrating the sensor's high reproducibility. Based on the slope of the analytical curve, the developed sensor's sensitivity was calculated to be 179.7 μA ng/mL.

The Cu0-OGQ electrode was used in the repeatability and stability experiments, which involved measuring the maximum peak fluctuations of the electrode throughout a range of storage times while it was kept in a sealed environment at 5 °C and subjected to repeated reuse cycles. The current response of the electrode was studied for various storage times that lasted for 30 days to gain insight into the long-term stability of the Cu0-OGQ/GCE. According to the results, which are depicted in Fig. 7c, the peak current only deviated mainly after 28 days and the peak response remained the same after the 30th day, demonstrating the remarkable stability of the Cu0-OGQ/GCE electrode. The remarkable repeatability of the fabricated electrode is demonstrated by the minimal variance between the peak current responses, as illustrated in Fig. 7c.

Average RSDs of less than 3 % were obtained when Cu0-OGQ/GCE was used to examine the impact of potential interferents on the selectivity of Glyp. The result shows that different types of interference had very little impact on Glyp (Fig. 7d), demonstrating the electrode's strong anti-interference capabilities. The major metabolite of Glyp, AMPA, had no significant impact on the electrochemical sensing of Glyp, however, an obvious antagonistic effect was observed in the presence of Ace. The findings presented here are in line with research that Cao et al. [57] published. Based on free energy values from DFT calculations, the scientists found that coordinating acetochlor and thiram pesticides with Cu2+ in the Cu-BTC MOF-based electrochemical sensor is difficult.

The Cu0-OGQ/GCE sensor is severely unaffected by the presence of the cations that are typically found in water samples, however, Ni2+ did exhibit a spike in the anodic peak current, which can be linked to the antagonistic complexation of Ni2+ with Glyp. Low values of theRSDssuggest there were no noticeable variations in the sensor response under a variety of test conditions. The Cu0-OGQ/GCE can therefore be utilised in analytical studiesto detect Glyp residues across different environmental samples.

Glyphosate detection poses a significant challenge due to its metal-chelating properties. However, the results presented in Fig. 8 indicate that the presence of interferents such as iron and potassium salts in vegetables and fruits has a negligible impact on the performance of the Cu0-OGQ Glyp sensor when detecting glyphosate in these samples. The Cu0-OGQ electrode is capable of distinguishing glyphosate from phenolic compounds, despite their structural similarities, resulting in an average RSD of 2.65 %. This result demonstrates the electrode's robust anti-interference capabilities in the presence of both organic and inorganic interferents. In conclusion, the Cu0-OGQ Glyp sensor exhibits strong performance in detecting glyphosate in fruits and vegetables, making it a suitable choice for glyphosate detection in various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of Cu0-OGQ to environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed Cu0-OGQ electrochemical sensor was utilized to detect Glyp in various environmental samples using an analytical approach, and the findings were compared with those obtained using high-performanceliquid chromatography (HPLC) [78] and GC–MS as an established technique to determine the applicability of the sensor. The environmental samples were spiked with various concentrations of Glyp standard solutions as described in sections 2.6–2.8 and Glyp was found by DPV measurements utilizing the developed Cu0-OGQ sensor under optimal conditions.

The analytical findings are shown in Table 3 , and for the Cu0-OGQ electrode, the recoveries of Glyp from environmental samples ranged from 90 to 102 %, while the RSD% was within the range of 0.3–2.5 %. Based on the t-test evaluation with p < 0.05, there was no appreciable difference between the quantities of Glyp in real samples measured by HPLC, GC–MS, and those detected by the developed Cu0-OGQ electrochemical sensor (Table S1). These findings showed that the Cu0-OGQ electrochemical sensor is highly accurate and reliable for the detection of Glyp in actual samples with good recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: A facile method was used to synthesize graphitic carbon quantum dots (OGQ) from discarded orange peel. Cu0 nanoparticles were then doped onto the OGQ to produce Cu0-OGQ. Physico-chemical (FTIR, XRD, BET, XPS, Raman spectroscopy) and electrochemical (EIS) techniques were used to characterise Cu0-OGQ. According to the findings of the characterization, the dopant (Cu0) enhanced the OGQ's electrochemical active area, conductivity, and performance. The Cu0-OGQ electrochemical sensor demonstrated good sensitivity, selectivity, and identification of Glyp under optimal conditions. It also displayed outstanding stability even after 30 days.

Compared to numerous previously reported sensors, the Cu0–OGQ electrochemical sensor had a wider linear range, high sensitivity, and a low LOD for Glyp. The sensor demonstrated significant recovery rates in the range of 90 % to 102 % with an RSD of 0.2–2.5 % when used to practically detect Glyp in a variety of vegetables, fruits, water, and soil samples. The results obtained using the HPLC standard procedure and the Cu0–OGQ sensor did not significantly differ from one another. Given Cu0-OGQ's high sensitivity, low detection limit, and satisfactory selectivity, the newly developed sensor shows outstanding potential for effectively streamlining the detection of Glyp herbicide residues in environmental samples.
